critique.summary: The article claims that the open problem related to improving lower bounds on a sequence H(n) in Ramsey theory has been solved using GPT-5.4 Pro, with confirmation from a contributing mathematician, Will Brian. The solution is planned to be written up for publication, and other AI models have also solved the problem using a developed testing scaffold.
mainPoints: Kevin Barreto and Liam Price used GPT-5.4 Pro to find a solution to a frontier math open problem involving hypergraphs and Ramsey theory.
Will Brian, a contributor to the problem, confirmed the solution and expressed interest in publishing it and exploring follow-on work.
Other AI models such as Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4 (xhigh) also solved the problem using a general testing scaffold.
The problem involves improving lower bounds on H(n), related to partitions in hypergraphs, which has been a challenging open problem in mathematics.
The solution and related AI interactions are documented and will be updated with future publications.
critique.truthfulness: The claim that GPT-5.4 Pro solved a known open problem in Ramsey theory is extraordinary and currently lacks independent verification from established mathematical journals or multiple independent expert sources. While the article states that a contributing mathematician confirmed the solution and plans to publish it, there is no evidence yet of peer-reviewed publication or broader community acceptance. The involvement of multiple AI models in solving the problem is plausible given recent advances in AI-assisted mathematics, but the claim should be treated cautiously until formal publication and peer review occur.
considerations: The article does not provide direct links to the full solution or peer-reviewed publications verifying the claim.
No independent expert opinions or external validations beyond the contributor Will Brian are presented.
The complexity and significance of the problem suggest that independent verification by the mathematical community is necessary before accepting the claim.
The article does not discuss potential limitations or errors in the AI-generated solution.
The broader implications for AI in mathematical research and the reproducibility of the solution by other researchers are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the recent claim that GPT-5.4 Pro solved a frontier math open problem, with commenters debating the significance, accuracy, and implications of this achievement. Some express excitement about AI's potential in advancing mathematics, while others question the novelty and human involvement in the process.

comments.author: vlinx
comments.text: This is a remarkable result if confirmed independently. The gap between solving competition problems and open research problems has always been significant - bridging that gap suggests something qualitatively different in the model capabilities.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the importance of independently verifying the claim and the significance of AI bridging the gap between competition problems and open research problems.

comments.author: EternalFury
comments.text:
I am thinking there’s a large category of problems that can be solved by resampling existing proofs.
It’s the kind of brute force expedition machine can attempt relentlessly where humans would go mad trying.
It probably doesn’t really advance the field, but it can turn conjectures into theorems.
comments.analysis: The comment provides a reasonable critique that AI may be recombining existing knowledge rather than truly advancing mathematics, which is a valid perspective on current AI capabilities.

comments.author: pugio
comments.text:
I've never yet been "that guy" on HN but... the title seems misleading. The actual title is "A Ramsey-style Problem on Hypergraphs" and a more descriptive title would be "All latest frontier models can solve a frontier math open problem". (It wasn't just GPT 5.4)

Super cool, of course.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out that multiple models solved the problem and that the headline might overemphasize GPT-5.4 Pro's role, providing useful clarification.

comments.author: qnleigh
comments.text:
Their 'Open Problems page' linked below gives some interesting context. They list 15 open problems in total, categorized as 'moderately interesting,' 'solid result,' 'major advance,' or 'breakthrough.' The solved problem is listed as 'moderately interesting,' which is presumably the easiest category. But it's notable that the problem was selected and posted here before it was solved. I wonder how long until the other 3 problems in this category are solved.

https://epoch.ai/frontiermath/open-problems
comments.analysis: This comment adds useful context about the problem's difficulty ranking on the source site, tempering expectations about the significance of this particular solved problem.

comments.author: Validark
comments.text: I have long said I am an AI doubter until AI could print out the answers to hard problems or ones requiring tons of innovation. Assuming this is verified to be correct (not by AI) then I just became a believer. I would like to see a few more AI inventions to know for sure, but wow, it really is a new and exciting world. I really hope we use this intelligence resource to make the world better.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a cautious optimism contingent on independent verification, reflecting a common and reasonable stance in the community.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
I was trying to get Claude and Codex to try and write a proof in Isabelle for the Collatz conjecture, but annoyingly it didn't solve it, and I don't feel like I'm any closer than I was when I started. AI is useless!

In all seriousness, this is pretty cool. I suspect that there's a lot of theoretical math that haven't been solved simply because of the "size" of the proof. An AI feedback loop into something like Isabelle or Lean does seem like it could end up opening up a lot of proofs.
comments.analysis: The comment reflects personal experience with AI limitations but acknowledges the significance of the current achievement, showing a balanced perspective.

comments.author: letmetweakit
comments.text: Impressive, but it will take away so much sense of accomplishment from so many people. I find that really sad.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses an emotional concern about the impact of AI achievements on human motivation, which is a subjective but valid social perspective.

comments.author: bandrami
comments.text: It's deeply surprising to me that LLMs have had more success proving higher math theorems than making successful consumer software
comments.analysis: The comment offers an interesting observation about AI performance domains, though it is somewhat anecdotal and speculative.

comments.author: virgildotcodes
comments.text:
I don't know why I am still perpetually shocked that the default assumption is that humans are somehow unique.

It's this pervasive belief that underlies so much discussion around what it means to be intelligent. The null hypothesis goes out the window.

People constantly make comments like "well it's just trying a bunch of stuff until something works" and it seems that they do not pause for a moment to consider whether or not that also applies to humans.

If they do, they apply it in only the most restrictive way imaginable, some 2 dimensional caricature of reality, rather than considering all the ways that humans try and fail in all things throughout their lifetimes in the process of learning and discovery.

There's still this seeming belief in magic and human exceptionalism, deeply held, even in communities that otherwise tend to revolve around the sciences and the empirical.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a philosophical reflection on human exceptionalism and intelligence, relevant but less directly tied to the technical discussion.

comments.author: jvdsf
comments.text: Really? He was steering the wheel the whole time. GPT didn't do the math.
comments.analysis: This comment suggests significant human involvement in the process, a reasonable caution against over-attributing credit to AI.
